name: Wakea
description: Wakea, a prominent figure in Polynesian cosmogony, particularly within Hawaiian traditions, is often understood as the Sky Father. He is a foundational ancestor, credited with the creation of the islands and the establishment of social order. Wakea's significance extends beyond mere creation; he embodies the principle of light and the celestial realm. His union with Papa, the Earth Mother, is a recurring theme representing the fundamental duality and interconnectedness of the universe. He is considered a source of mana, spiritual power, and his influence is woven into the fabric of Hawaiian culture, influencing governance, agriculture, and genealogical narratives. Wakea's lineage is traced back to the primordial void, emphasizing his role as a bridge between the formless and the formed, the potential and the actual. Rituals and chants often invoke his name, acknowledging his continuing presence and impact on the natural world and human society. His stories serve as both historical accounts and moral frameworks, guiding principles for navigating the complexities of life and maintaining harmony with the environment.
subtitle: Sky Father

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Islands
keyMyths.description: Wakea, in his divine union with Papa, the Earth Mother, is credited with the creation of the Hawaiian islands. Through their sacred relationship, new lands emerged from the sea, each island representing a different aspect of their divine partnership. The islands are considered children of Wakea and Papa, imbued with their mana and reflecting the balance of the cosmos. These creation myths are central to the Hawaiian understanding of their origins and connection to the land.
keyMyths.source: Beckwith, Martha Warren. *Hawaiian Mythology*. University of Hawaii Press, 1970.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Haloa
keyMyths.description: Wakea and his daughter Hoʻohokukalani conceived Haloanakalaukapalili, a stillborn child. From his grave grew the first kalo (taro) plant, a staple food and sacred symbol in Hawaiian culture. Later, Wakea and Hoʻohokukalani conceived Haloa, the ancestor of all Hawaiians. This myth illustrates the cycle of life, death, and rebirth, highlighting the importance of agriculture and familial lineage in Hawaiian society. It also connects Wakea directly to the sustenance and well-being of his people.
keyMyths.source: Kamakau, Samuel Manaiakalani. *Ruling Chiefs of Hawaii*. Kamehameha Schools Press, 1992.

keyMyths.title: The Separation of Wakea and Papa
keyMyths.description: Initially, Wakea and Papa were inseparable, their union resulting in constant creation. However, their relationship became strained, leading to a separation. This separation, while causing sorrow, allowed for the development of seasons, the differentiation of day and night, and the emergence of distinct land formations. The separation is not seen as a complete severing but rather a necessary stage in the ongoing cycle of creation and renewal, allowing for greater complexity and diversity in the world.
keyMyths.source: Malo, David. *Hawaiian Antiquities*. Bishop Museum Press, 1951.

sources.author: Beckwith, Martha Warren
